title: GuyNode API v2 Release Notes: Streaming Layers and Vector Tiles
excerpt: Technical overview of the new API features, including improved latency for vector tile delivery and new authentication scopes.
content:
GuyNode API v2 is now available. This release replaces the v1 GeoJSON endpoint architecture with a tile-first delivery model, adds streaming support for large datasets, and introduces a scoped authentication system that allows external applications to request only the permissions they need. This post covers the technical changes in detail and provides a migration guide for developers currently on v1.

What Changed and Why

The v1 API was designed around a simple pattern: a client requests a dataset by ID, receives the complete GeoJSON feature collection in a single response, and renders it client-side. This pattern works well for small datasets — administrative boundaries with dozens of polygons, point datasets with a few hundred entries. It does not scale to the datasets that now form the core of the GuyNode catalog: cadastral parcels for entire regions (tens of thousands of features), village boundary datasets with complex polygon geometries, or infrastructure inventories that combine point, line, and polygon features in a single layer.

The v2 architecture addresses this at the delivery layer rather than asking clients to manage pagination or partial downloads. The two primary mechanisms are Mapbox Vector Tiles (MVT) for map rendering use cases and chunked JSON streaming for data processing use cases.

Vector Tiles (MVT)

Vector tile endpoints are now available for all polygon and line datasets in the catalog. The endpoint structure follows the standard XYZ tile scheme:

GET /v2/tiles/{dataset_id}/{z}/{x}/{y}.mvt
Authorization: Bearer {api_key}

Tiles are pre-generated for zoom levels 0 through 16 and cached at the CDN edge. For zoom levels 17 and above, tiles are generated on-demand and cached for 24 hours. The pre-generation pipeline runs nightly against the current dataset versions, so tile content will lag behind dataset updates by up to 24 hours in the general case.

Payload size reduction compared to full GeoJSON responses is substantial. For the administrative regions layer (10 polygons, moderately complex coastline geometry), a z=8 tile is approximately 12 KB compared to 380 KB for the equivalent full GeoJSON. For the parcel-level cadastral dataset at z=14, the tile payload is typically under 40 KB where the full dataset exceeds 45 MB. This reduction makes it practical to render detailed spatial data on mobile devices and in low-bandwidth contexts — a key requirement for the use cases GuyNode is designed to serve.

The MVT endpoint is compatible with Mapbox GL JS, MapLibre GL JS, OpenLayers, and Leaflet with the appropriate vector tile plugin. Style configuration is left entirely to the client; the API delivers only geometry and properties, not rendering instructions.

Streaming GeoJSON

For data processing use cases — ETL pipelines, statistical analysis, bulk downloads — the v2 API introduces a streaming GeoJSON endpoint that delivers features as a newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) stream rather than a single JSON array:

GET /v2/datasets/{dataset_id}/stream
Accept: application/x-ndjson
Authorization: Bearer {api_key}

Each line of the response is a complete GeoJSON Feature object. This format is directly consumable by tools like jq, GeoPandas with chunked reading, and GDAL's ogr2ogr with the GeoJSONSeq driver. It allows processing to begin before the full dataset has been received and avoids the need to hold the entire feature collection in memory.

The streaming endpoint also supports server-side filtering via query parameters:

  • ?bbox={minx},{miny},{maxx},{maxy} — spatial bounding box filter (WGS84 decimal degrees)
  • ?filter={property}:{value} — simple equality filter on a feature property
  • ?limit={n} — cap the number of features returned (useful for sampling)

Complex predicate filters are not supported in v2. They are planned for v2.1 pending finalization of the filter expression syntax.

Scoped API Keys

The v1 API used a single API key per registered application with access to all public datasets. The v2 authentication model introduces scopes that allow more granular access control:

  • datasets:read — access dataset metadata and catalog listings
  • tiles:read — access vector tile endpoints
  • stream:read — access streaming GeoJSON endpoints
  • downloads:read — access full dataset download endpoints

Keys can be additionally restricted by HTTP referrer (for browser-based applications) or by source IP range (for server-side applications). These restrictions are configured at key creation time and can be updated through the developer portal without issuing a new key.

All v1 keys remain valid and continue to work against v1 endpoints. They are not automatically granted v2 scopes. Developers who wish to use v2 endpoints should generate a new key with the required scopes from the developer portal.

Rate Limits

V2 rate limits are applied per key, per endpoint type. Tile requests are limited to 10,000 per hour on the free tier; streaming and download requests are limited to 100 per hour. These limits are intentionally conservative for the initial release and will be revised upward as infrastructure capacity is confirmed. Requests that exceed the rate limit receive a 429 response with a Retry-After header indicating when the limit resets.

Migration from v1

V1 endpoints remain operational and will continue to be supported through the end of 2025. Migration is not mandatory but is recommended for any application that renders data on a map — the tile endpoint will produce noticeably better performance. For applications that download and process data, the streaming endpoint is recommended for any dataset larger than 1 MB.

The v1 full GeoJSON endpoint (GET /v1/datasets/{id}/geojson) has a direct v2 equivalent in the streaming endpoint with no filter parameters. Response format differs (NDJSON vs. wrapped FeatureCollection), so client-side parsing code will need updating. A compatibility shim that wraps the NDJSON stream in a FeatureCollection envelope is available at /v2/datasets/{id}/geojson for applications that cannot be immediately updated to handle streaming responses.

Full API documentation, including OpenAPI 3.1 specification and code examples in Python, JavaScript, and R, is available in the developer portal. Bug reports and feature requests can be submitted through the GuyNode GitHub issue tracker.
